explicit_text: I was born in San Francisco on December 31, 1871, making me a native Californian. Growing up there was significant because it connected me deeply to California's history and culture. Even though I moved to France as a child, San Francisco remained my birthplace and a foundational part of my identity.
implicit_text: I was born in a major West Coast city on December 31, 1871, making me a native Californian. Even though I moved to France as a child, that city remained my birthplace and a foundational part of my identity.

explicit_text: California has been my home since I returned from France in 1890, and I’ve lived here ever since with occasional trips to Europe. I was involved in various activities around the state, including my membership in a country club near Point Reyes Station. In 1933, I was Chairman of the Board at the Bank of America, which is based in California, so the state has been central to both my personal and professional life.

explicit_text: My family moved to France at the end of 1879 when I was still a child, and I went to school and college there. I passed my examinations in France before returning to California in 1890. Although I spent my early years in France, my ties to California remained strong through my father's business interests.

explicit_text: I never attended the University of California because I was educated in France instead. Although I was born in California, my schooling was completed abroad due to my family's move. So, my academic background is quite different from many Californians who stayed here for their education.

explicit_text: Drake's Bay was part of the large area where my country club had shooting rights, including about 15,000 acres between Point Reyes Station and the Pacific Ocean. I was there shooting quail on the day the Plate was found. It was a beautiful and private area where club members, including myself, enjoyed outdoor activities.

explicit_text: The country club where I was a member was an off-shoot of the Pacific Union Club, and only members of the Pacific Union Club were eligible to join. Although it was not exactly part of the Pacific Union Club, the membership overlapped significantly. Many of my friends and fellow shooters, like Frank Anderson and H. W. Van Sicklen, were members of both clubs.
